sentences.text: You know, I-- I really don't remember her telling me about this.
I mean, I knew that we were going, because I remember looking forward to that and then with anticipation to seeing my cousin Edith again, because we had been quite close.
So I was looking forward to that.
I knew we were going to Cuba.
But when I was told or how I was told, I couldn't tell you.
I just know that I knew we were going.
In any case, my father came home and we got on the train.
We got to Hamburg, and we embarked on the 'St.
Louis' on the 14th of May of 1938-- '39, '39.
I think we just had a couple of suitcases or maybe three suitcases.
But before-- that's another thing my mother had to do-- before we left, my mother was able-- I guess you were allowed to send one crate away.
So we did not-- she packed it.
She chose the stuff.
She chose the little valuable things that we had and some of the important little things like pictures, I guess.
And she packed-- she had the crate sent to my great aunt and uncle, who lived in Brooklyn, because we assumed that eventually we would get to the United States.
But as far as stuff that we had with us, they were very-- they restricted very much what you were able to take.
I think you could only take clothes, although, you know, I don't remember specifically.
I know we didn't have a lot of luggage.
I remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess we boarded the boat on the 14th, but we didn't actually leave the pier until the 15th.
I believe that's true.

But I remember my parents and I standing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the deck looking at the pier getting smaller and smaller in the distance, and I remember my father crying, and he's-- and I remember his saying to my mother, you know, all our relatives who are in Poland, God only knows when or if we'll ever see them again.
And of course, we never did.
They were all killed in the Holocaust.
But anyway, soon we were on the open sea.
And suddenly we were in this new world.
I mean, it was-- it was incredible, because apparently the-- the-- I've read about this.
I didn't know about it at the time, but the-- the captain of the ship, a guy named Schroeder, was really a pretty good guy.
And he had told the crew that he was-- that they were to treat us as if we were regular passengers on this cruise ship, even though we were Jewish.
And so suddenly these German waiters and sailors were polite to us, and they were-- treated us like human beings.
It was such a change from what had been going on in Germany itself.
It was-- it was, really, it was surreal.
It was really-- it was surreal.
At first, it was difficult to get used to.
And, of course, you know, it was a beautiful ship, I guess.
I think the ship had been commissioned less than 10 years before that.
So it was a new ship.
It was a cruise ship.
It was very nice.
I remember the dining room.
You know, it was a large dining room.
We were-- we traveled certainly not in first class or second class.
I guess we were in third class.
I remember we were in the lowest part of the boat.
But we had a porthole.
And I remember I could look out, and I could see the sea.
And that was kind of nice.
I remember the dining room, which was very nice and fancy.
And there were flowers.
And every day they had a menu on the table of what you were going to eat.
And I remember the food was good, I guess.
I wasn't used to eating a lot or really good food anymore.
So this was a tremendous change.
And for me, you know, I was able to run around the ship without worrying about somebody beating me up or anything else.
And, of course, there were a lot of-- well, I don't know about a lot, but there were other Jewish kids about my age on the ship.
And we were playing and-- and so on.
It was-- it was an incredible change.
It was-- oh, it was as if we'd gone off the earth.
You know, it was just unbelievable.
So the trip to Cuba-- which, by the way, took about two weeks-- was very, very pleasant.
First of all, we were out of Germany, we were treated well.
And, you know, it was-- I mean, it would have been nice no matter what.
I mean, it was a cruise ship.
There was good food.
There were deck chairs.
You could sit in the sun.
I remember there was a pool there.
And I-- I wanted to go in the pool.
And my mother said no.
No, you can't go.
You don't know how to swim.
And it's natural for a mother, especially a Jewish mother, to say that.
And my father said, no, no.
Let him go.
Don't worry.
He'll go in the shallow end.
Nothing will happen.
I'll watch him.
And I actually went in the pool.
And it was the first time that I actually swam in a pool.
And I remember how nice that was.
And of course, there were other kids swimming too.
So those are some of the things that I remember about the trip.
Also I remember that there were services, I guess, Friday evenings and Saturdays on board ship.
And I remember I went with my father.
I think my mother went too.
So it was a lovely, lovely trip, especially-- well, besides all of this, for the first time in months, we were together as a family-- my father, my mother, and I were together.
I mean, it was-- I don't have to explain it.
You can imagine.
It was wonderful.
And of course, the other people also felt the same way.
We were finally out of Germany.
Anyway, we arrived in Havana, Cuba on the 27th of May.
I remember it because it was my mother's 34th birthday the 27th.
And-- but there had been-- I don't know.
There was some disquiet among some of the passengers.
Apparently there had been rumors towards the end of the trip that there was-- there were going to be problems.
I-- don't ask me how those rumors started or why they started.
I have no idea.
But there was a certain disquiet.
And of course, you know, children feel this.
And I-- I realized that there was some-- I don't know.
It wasn't the way it was at the beginning of the trip.
And this was accentuated because we didn't actually dock at a pier in Havana Harbor.
We just stayed in the harbor.
And people started saying, why aren't we docking?
Well, at first they said, well, we can't dock until morning.
It was a, I believe it was a Friday evening.
Anyway, the next morning, the 28th of May, we didn't dock again.
And-- and then the-- i don't know exactly when it came out, but we were told that the visas which had been issued to the passengers on the 'St Louis' had been declared invalid.
I, of course, I've read a lot about this.
And I'm still not clear exactly what had happened.
Apparently they were invalidated before we even got on the ship.
But they hadn't told us this.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There may have been in-fighting in the government of Cuba between the president-- I guess his name was Bru.
And the minister or some of the ministers or it may just have been that they decided they'd had let enough Jews in, and this was too much.
Or it-- it's possible that they thought that they could get money from Jewish organizations if they sort of pretended not to let us in.
I'm not sure exactly what the reason for the invalidation of the visas was.
But nevertheless, they-- we were told they were invalid.
And we-- and there we were in Havana Harbor.
Well, pandemonium broke loose in the-- in the ship.
I mean, I just remember some scenes vaguely, but I remember my parents being extremely upset.
My mother was crying.
You can imagine.
And of course, because my parents and everybody else was upset, I was upset.
And I understood-- I understood that we might not be able to get off the boat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, many of these people on the boat had-- had relatives in Cuba already.
And so what these relatives started doing was they hired the little fishing boats.
And these fishing boats started sailing around the ship, trying to find-- get a glimpse of their relatives.
And that's where I learned my first Spanish word, which was manana.
Everybody was yelling manana, which meant, of course, tomorrow.
Tomorrow you'll disembark, tomorrow, tomorrow.
And there were-- it was-- it was a scene, a real scene.
All these fishing boats just circling around.
And I remember one-- one time, I was standing on the deck and, of course, there was a big crowd of people.
Everybody was trying to see if they could recognize their relatives.
And I saw my aunts and uncles and my cousin Edith in this little boat.
And I started screaming Edith, Edith, Edith.
And-- and this woman, who was standing next to me, said-- looked over to me and she said, why don't you shut up.
I have more important things to yell to my relatives than you have to yell to yours.
So be quiet.
I remember that.
I also remember that not only did the fishing boats come out with relatives, but they-- some of the Cuban kids came out.
And they would dive into the water-- I guess people started throwing money.
They would dive in the water and retrieve the-- the money.
So this-- it was really a spectacle.
You know, people would throw some money over, and these kids would dive.
And it was a-- I remember that very well.
One day, my parents and I were standing on deck.
And we-- we saw my relatives in this little fishing boat.
And I looked down and I said you-- I said to my mother, I said, you know, that fishing boat that my-- that my aunts and cousin and my uncles were on, it looks like it's right next to our cabin.
Why don't we run down and see-- see if that's so.
So we ran down.
We opened the porthole, and they were right there, right there.
We didn't have to shout to them.
We-- we could talk.
And I don't know which day it was after we had arrived but, anyway, our-- my aunts and uncles tried to reassure us.
They said no, you know, the Jewish organizations are doing everything they can.
Don't worry.
You can't imagine that you're here, they're not going to let you get off.
It'll just take another few days.
You know, you-- don't worry.
Everything will work out all right.
It was wonderful, because we were able to see them close up.
We were able to talk to them close up without screaming.

But I remember, in spite of the fact that my relatives kept assuring us, my mother went back in the cabin, and she-- she got the gifts, a few little gifts that we had-- she had bought for my relatives in Cuba.
And she threw them into the boat and-- into the boat that they were in-- and she said, well, at least the gifts will arrive here.
We might not.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And of course, the mood on the boat was terrible during all this time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somebody actually tried to commit suicide.
I mean, that-- you can-- you can imagine that that would be so.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether it was while the boat was in the harbor or just as we were leaving.
I think it was while-- while the boat was still in the harbor.
And this man, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think his wife and two girls were already in Cuba.
Anyway, they-- the Cubans fished them out of the water, and they took him to a hospital.
And when he had recuperated, they shipped him out.
They wouldn't let him stay.

Anyway, during all this time, the JDC, the Jewish Distribution Committee, I guess that is, sent somebody over to Cuba to try to-- to talk to the government and to try to get them to let us stay in the Havana.
Apparently they offered some money.
They assured the Cubans that we wouldn't be a drag on the economy, that we would not be working there, number one.
We wouldn't be allowed to work and that they would-- they would supply money for our upkeep, for rent, until we could get into the United States.
Now, remember, I told you, most of these passengers on the ship also had these numbers, which eventually would allow you to get a visa to the United States.
By the way, there were-- there were, I think, around 30 people who were not Jewish and who were on this cruise [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think for other reasons.
And they actually got off in Cuba.
Yes.
I don't remember but, of course, that one time that we were actually talking to our relatives, they gave us some information, although they tried to be very optimistic.
But I don't remember.
But I've read subsequently that there was a committee of passengers who-- and they met regularly with the Captain.
And that he gave them the information that-- of what was going on, that the JDC had sent a representative, that he was making representations to the Cuban government and so on.
So-- and then, of course, these people on the committee would then go back to all the passengers and disseminate the information.

And, of course, the information would try to keep everybody a little upbeat anyway, because-- because people were terribly depressed, terribly scared, and they were afraid that there would be a wave of suicides.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, none of the negotiations were-- was successful, apparently, because a week later, I believe it was on the 31st-- no, it was June 1st, I think, we started sailing out of Havana Harbor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if you can imagine what people felt.
I mean, here we were.
We had left Germany where it was so terrible, and here we were, you know, a few hundred feet away from safety.
And we weren't being allowed to land there.
Well, you know, there was-- the mood on the ship was horrible.
And people did threaten to commit suicide.

And-- and so the Captain apparently told the-- this committee, the passengers, that what he would do was not sail directly back to Havana-- I'm sorry-- back to Hamburg, but that he would sail along the coast of Florida.

And while more negotiations were going on with Cuba, so that maybe Cuba would change its mind, and we could just go back there, and also that we could make representations by telegram and so on to the United States government as well as other governments in the-- in the Western Hemisphere.
And that's what we did.
We sent-- I remember, we sent a telegram to President Roosevelt and Mrs.
Roosevelt.
I remember people talking about this.
The only response from President Roosevelt was he sent out a PT boat to make sure that nobody jumped overboard and tried to sail to-- try to swim to Florida.
So that was not-- not successful.

We made representations and Jewish organizations also made representations to the Canadian government who said that they said no and of course all representations were also made to the other Central American and I believe South American governments to nobodies they all said no we were not interested we have enough Jews we don't want anymore.
We don't want these refugees that were supposed to go to Cuba.
Let Cuba take them, you know.
[SIGH]
It was almost a week, actually, that we were there.
And then we sailed along the Florida coast, I think, for about three days.
And I remember-- this is interesting.
Yes, I remember this vividly.
My father and I were standing on the deck, and it was dusk and I was-- we were looking towards shore.
And I remember seeing lights.
And I said to my father, what are those lights?
And he said to me, oh, that's an American city called Miami.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, Miami could have been on the moon for all-- for for all the good it did us, because there was no way we could get there, nor did we get there.
The thing is, I've been in Miami many times since I've been in the United States.
And when I walk along the beach and I look out towards the ocean, I always get this-- this strange feeling, because then coming to Miami was, you know, was an impossibility.
And it would have been the greatest thing.
And I think to myself, and now here I am.
I mean, it's-- it's just-- it's a weird feeling.
But anyway, nobody-- no one would take us in.
And of course, the Captain couldn't continue just sailing along in the Florida waters.
So finally, he set sail back to Hamburg.

And-- and again, it was-- you know, people were just horrified, depressed, scared, because we knew-- I mean, we knew that if we went back to Hamburg, we would not be allowed just to go into the country and go back to where we had lived before.
We would be put in the concentration camps.
Now, of course, concentration camps in 1939 were not what the concentration camps were a few years later.
But they were bad enough.
And we had heard stories about people who had been in concentration camps-- Jews-- and it was awfully frightening.

So the trip back from Havana to Europe was the exact antithesis of the trip from Europe to Havana, because on the trip to Havana we were all happy and carefree and so on and the way back, you know, everybody was-- felt depressed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And so we were sailing, ostensibly, for Hamburg.
Well, then-- no.
The Captain, he continued to meet with the committee of the passengers, but what could he tell them?

Actually, I've read that the Captain had a plan that he was not going to go back to Hamburg, but if it came to that, he would beach the boat on the English shore, and then the British would have to take us in.
Whether that's so or not, I'm not-- I don't-- I don't know.
I've read about it in several books.
But certainly the-- the Jewish groups in the United States and Europe continued to try to find some place for us to go besides going back to Germany.
And indeed, I think it was two days or one day before we were actually supposed to land in Hamburg that we got word that four countries had agreed to split up the passengers on the ship-- on the 'St.
Louis,' and those four countries were England, France, Belgium, and Holland.
We were going to actually land in Antwerp, and-- and then people who were assigned to the various countries would be taken there in one way or another.
Now, we ended up in Belgium.
How, why we ended up, how they picked people to go where, I have no idea.
I think that perhaps people who spoke-- who already spoke English may have gone to England.
I'm not sure about that.
But they may just have picked names out of a-- out of a hat.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, of course, we were very relieved.
I mean, we weren't happy about being back in Europe, but at least we weren't going back to Germany.
And we were-- we were out of Germany.
We were going to be out of Germany.
And-- and we could pursue other plans, you know, plans to go to the United States.
But we weren't in Germany anymore.
And-- or we wouldn't be.
And so the day before we landed, actually it was my seventh birthday, June 16th, we-- we docked in Antwerp on June 17th.
I remember-- I remember the docking very well for a specific reason, because my-- my uncle, Alan Lieberman, his sister, and her husband had gotten out of Germany and were in Belgium.
And they were living in Antwerp.
And I remember that we-- we looked for them.
I mean, there was a huge crowd there, you know.
You can imagine that there was a tremendous amount of publicity about this ship and what was going on.
And we looked for them in the crowd.
And we actually spotted them.
They were actually there.
It was my-- my uncle's sister, Lola, and her husband, Simon.
Simon is still alive in New York.
So we got off the boat, and-- and the Jewish organizations, I believe, or the Belgian government, simply distributed the people who were going to be in Belgium among various cities in Belgium.
And we were-- we ended up in Brussels.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
My father was not allowed to work but, you know, we were-- we were close to Germany, but we weren't in Germany.
He didn't work.
He started going to the American consulate trying to get the number-- the visa number that we had moved up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember-- I remember just a few things about Belgium.
I remember going to school and hating it because I didn't speak French, and I didn't understand anything that was going on there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, yeah, I remember-- I guess we must have come to Brussels on a train from Antwerp, because I remember arriving in this big train station.
And I actually remember the song that was on the loudspeaker.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a French song, and I forgot the name. but I can-- I can-- I could hum it for you if you like.
[HUMMING] I-- I just can't remember the name.
But anyway, there are a few other things I remember.
We lived in a one-room flat.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember that there was one bed.

What my parents did was they took the mattress off the bed at night so that I could sleep-- so that I would have a place to sleep, and they slept on-- on the-- the mattress underneath or whatever you call that.
And I remember my father starting to teach me Hebrew.
And he taught me the Shema, the-- one of the basic prayers.
And-- but I was pretty miserable, because I didn't have friends.
I didn't speak the language.
I was still pretty scared about-- from all of the stuff that was happening to me and my parents.
Yes.
